text: The concept of mental hygiene predates modern neuroscience but has found rigorous grounding in sleep research. William James, writing in 1890, argued that habits are fixed in the nervous system through repetition and rest, not effort alone. Contemporary sleep science confirms this intuition: procedural skills — playing an instrument, typing, athletic movements — improve measurably overnight without further practice, a phenomenon called offline learning. The improvement is not due to muscle repair but to neural replay in the motor cortex during sleep. This means that the quality of tomorrow's performance is determined not just by how hard you practiced today, but by how well you sleep tonight.
question: What does the passage identify as the neural mechanism behind overnight improvement in procedural skills?

options.text: Muscle fibers repair micro-damage sustained during physical practice
The prefrontal cortex reorganizes motor plans during REM sleep
Neural replay in the motor cortex during sleep consolidates the skill
Dopamine released during deep sleep reinforces reward-based motor pathways
